title: Making a Mould and Deckle
subtitle: Building a matched frame pair for hand papermaking
excerpt: The mould and deckle are the primary tools of hand papermaking. The mould carries the mesh on which the sheet forms; the deckle sits on top to contain the slurry at the edges. Both must be the same interior dimension and fit together without gap. This technique covers frame construction, mesh choice, stretching and fixing, and building the matching deckle.
sourceNotes: Hunter, Dard. Old Papermaking. Chillicothe, Ohio: Mountain House Press, 1923. Chapter on mould construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The rigid framed mesh used to form and drain a handmade sheet. The pulp settles on the mesh surface as water drains through it.

An open frame, matching the mould in outer dimensions, that sits on top of the mould during sheet formation to contain the pulp at the edges. The deckle produces the characteristic untrimmed edge of handmade paper.

A plain woven fabric mesh used on a papermaking mould. It produces a sheet with no visible wire pattern. Contrast with laid mesh, which is made of parallel horizontal wires held by widely spaced vertical chains and leaves a characteristic pattern of lines in the finished sheet.

attrs.heading: Cutting safety
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

content.text: How a mould and deckle work
The
mould
is lowered into the vat at an angle, brought level under the surface, then lifted through the pulp-laden water. The fibres settle onto the mesh as the water drains away. The
deckle

rests on top of the mould, containing the slurry within its inner boundary while the sheet forms. When the mould is lifted clear of the vat, the deckle is removed first; its underside will have a thin fibrous edge, which becomes the characteristic untrimmed 'deckle edge' of the paper.

The two frames must be the same outer dimension but fit together without binding. A gap larger than 1 to 2 mm between their mating faces allows pulp to seep through and produces ragged edges that are thicker than the sheet body.
Materials for the frame

Both frames should be made from a stable hardwood or exterior-grade hardwood strip: oak, ash, or a dense tropical hardwood all work well. Softwood splits along the grain when wet repeatedly, which is a significant problem since the mould goes into water with every pull. Hardwood strip of 20 x 10 mm section is adequate for an A4 mould; 25 x 12 mm is better for A3 or anything that will see heavy use.

Avoid wood that has been treated with preservative; the chemicals leach into the vat and can affect fibre formation and sizing. Untreated wood, sealed after construction with several coats of oil-based varnish or exterior wood oil, is the correct choice.
Frame jointing

Cut the four sides of the mould frame to length, mitre-cutting the corners at 45 degrees. Mitre joints are not structurally the strongest, but they keep the mesh attachment edge continuous and make stretching the mesh easier. For added strength, cut two small corner braces from the same stock and glue them inside the corners on the underside of the mould only (not the deckle, which must remain flat-faced for a clean fit).

Glue the mitre joints with waterproof PVA wood glue and reinforce with a small brass or stainless steel angle bracket screwed to the underside. Do not use iron screws; they rust immediately and stain the paper a brown-orange colour.
Check the frame is square by measuring both diagonals. They should be equal within 2 mm. If not, apply clamping pressure diagonally across the longer diagonal while the glue sets.
Mesh choice: wove versus laid
The
wove mesh

is the practical choice for most home papermakers. A synthetic woven mesh (polyester or nylon window screen fabric) is widely available, food-safe, rot-resistant, and produces a sheet with no visible wire pattern. Mesh count (threads per centimetre) affects the fineness of the sheet surface: 20 to 30 threads per centimetre is typical for writing paper; finer mesh for thinner or smoother sheets.

Laid mesh is the traditional alternative: parallel horizontal wires held by spaced vertical 'chain' wires. It requires fabricating or sourcing a pre-made laid mould and is a specialist undertaking. The resulting sheet has a characteristic pattern of parallel lines (the laid lines) with wider-spaced crossing lines (the chain lines), visible when the sheet is held to light. Historical papers from Hunter's era were almost all laid-mould papers.
Stretching and tacking the mesh
Cut the mesh 3 to 4 cm larger than the outer frame on all sides. This gives enough material to stretch and tack without the mesh pulling free at the corners.
Lay the mesh flat on a clean surface. Centre the mould frame mesh-side down on top of it.
Fold the mesh up over one long side of the frame. Apply tension by pulling the mesh taut. Staple or tack at the centre of this side first, then work toward the corners.
Move to the opposite long side, apply tension (pulling away from the first side), staple centre first, then outward.
Repeat for both short sides, keeping tension on all four edges before completing the stapling.
Fold the corners neatly, like a parcel wrap, and staple flat. Trim the excess mesh close to the staples.
Apply a bead of waterproof sealant along the edge where the mesh meets the frame on the underside. This prevents pulp from working under the mesh edge and avoids a thick border on every sheet.
Building the deckle

The deckle is simply an open frame with the same outer dimensions as the mould. No mesh is needed. Cut and join the deckle from the same stock as the mould, using the same joint method. The deckle does not need corner bracing on the inside face, since that face must rest flat on the mould.

Test the fit by placing the deckle on the mould. It should sit flat without rocking and with a minimal gap at the joint. Press gently on each corner to confirm the fit is even. If one corner rocks, sand the high point lightly.

Seal both frames with two to three coats of oil-based varnish, covering all surfaces including the end grain at the corner joints. Allow to dry fully between coats. The end grain is the most vulnerable point; it absorbs water fastest and is where the wood is most likely to split after repeated wetting.
